steps.title: Identify the key numerical contrast about free-elections clauses
steps.content: The notes show that all fifty state constitutions contain voting rights clauses, but only thirty contain free-elections clauses. This gap—between universal and merely partial—is the defining fact about how free-elections clauses are distributed.

steps.title: Apply the contrast to the choices
steps.content:
Choice A uses exactly this contrast: it notes that while voting rights are conferred universally, free and fair elections “aren't mandated with the same consistency.” This turns the numerical difference into a meaningful generalization about free-elections clauses.

The other choices either contradict the numbers (B claims all states have free-elections clauses), omit the clauses from consideration (C), or shift to legislation (D).

That matches choice A — A is correct.
